text:
Launching & scaling new product categories is hard in DTC.

You have to map out new funnels, test new creative, and spend thousands more on ads.

Here's how I'd use SEO to help a footwear brand launch a new apparel line & become the category leader on Google in under 6 months: 

At this point, I'll assume that you've done enough research to determine that your existing footwear customers want you to make athletic wear like leggings, sweats, hoodies, training gear, etc. AND that adding these products will increase your average customer lifetime value. 

These are 2 necessary steps that enabled brands like Nike, Adidas, and Reebok to expand from footwear brands to full-blown athletic wear brands and become multi-billion dollar companies. 

If you did both of those, here's where I'd start: 

1) I'd first identify a purchase intent keyword for each category that the brand plans to launch.

For this example, let's say I want to rank a collection page for "compression leggings," one of the 3 new product categories the brand is launching.

2) Next, I'd optimize the collection for the keyword (compression leggings), adding it to the title/H1, meta title, meta description, URL slug, image alt text & file name, first sentence, and naturally a few times throughout the collection description.

(I'd repeat this process for all 3 product categories that are being launched, this is just one example for brevity)

3) Add this collection to the main navigation & footer menu. 

4) Identify any close variants of the keyword that I might also be able to rank for, assuming they're similar in competition. 

In this case, I found 'compression leggings for women' and 'compression leggings for men,' both of which have decent search volume and are low competition.

Assuming the brand planned to launch a men's & women's line of compression leggings, I'd create both of these collections as well and repeat steps 1-3 for each of them. 

I'd also link to them from the main 'compression leggings' collection. 

5) Once the collections are optimized, linked in the menus, and published, I'd identify middle & top of funnel keywords that customers are searching for as they shop for compression leggings. Keywords like: 

- benefits of compression leggings
- do compression leggings work? 
- can you sleep with compression leggings? 
- what are compression leggings/what do they do?
- do compression leggings help with cellulite? 
- do compression leggings help with leg cramps?
- are compression leggings good/bad for you?

6) I'd prioritize these based on funnel stage, search volume, and competition, then write blogs to target each of these keywords. 

I'd publish 4-6 of them each month until I covered the majority of the funnel, which would likely take less than 6 months for a product category as specific as this.

7) I would add an internal link from every single one of these blogs to the main collection. Specifically, it'd be the first internal link in each blog post and feature a keyword-specific anchor. 

8) I'd also link to the men's & women's collections if sections of each blog specifically addressed questions, problems, or concerns of men/women. 

9) Finally, I'd internally link to 4-5 of the other blogs using contextual anchors, ensuring at least every blog has 4+ internal links pointing to it, preventing any orphan pages. 

10) I'd regularly build 5-10 backlinks each month to the home page, blog posts, and collection pages using guest posts, link insertions, and press syndication. 

11 (Bonus): I'd integrate appropriate CTAs & email sign-ups on the blogs. 

I'd also suggest that the brand sets up Google Retargeting ads to continually serve ads to customers that viewed the blog content but didn't make a purchase.  

That's it - extremely simple & straightforward. Assuming you're already a somewhat established brand in the footwear space, you could likely rank #1 for 'compression leggings' and tons of supporting keywords within 6 months of launching the new apparel line. 

This would add tens of thousands of dollars to your monthly revenue, all without having to constantly pump out new creative or spend more money on paid ads.
